title: Acceptable Variance
description: Gerald has a wildflower growing in his front lawn. It is out of compliance with HOA lawn standards. He has tried mowing it, pulling it, four approved products, and — at his wife's suggestion — asking it politely to stop. It keeps growing back. He is not opposed to the flower on principle. Just operationally.

offer.speaker: Gerald
offer.text: I was wondering if you might be able to assist with a yard-related situation.

offer.speaker: Gerald
offer.text: There's a wildflower in my lawn. Out of compliance. Quarterly inspection's coming. I've mowed it, pulled it, applied all four approved products. My wife suggested asking it politely. She's not wrong that I hadn't tried everything.

offer.speaker: Gerald
offer.text: It grows back by morning. Every time. Three weeks now. I'm not opposed to the flower on principle, you understand. Just operationally.

accept.speaker: Gerald
accept.text: Anything you can figure out. I'll be here. Mowing around it, for now, which the HOA counts as 'partial compliance,' which is somehow worse than none.

decline.speaker: Gerald
decline.text: Understood. It's a lot to ask. It's one flower. It's just also, somehow, not.

in_progress.speaker: Gerald
in_progress.text: Mrs. Pemberton next door watches everything. Has since before the HOA. If anyone knows what the deal with that patch of lawn is, it's her.

ready_to_turn_in.speaker: Gerald
ready_to_turn_in.text: Form 44-Omega. You actually — that's the variance form. That's been granted twice in HOA history. You're holding a third one.

complete.speaker: Gerald
complete.text: I thought about the HOA's response a lot. They granted it because the field pre-dated the neighborhood. The flower has more right to be here than the lawn.

complete.speaker: Gerald
complete.text: That felt important somehow. I keep almost knowing why.

complete.speaker: Gerald
complete.text: Anyway. It stays. I'll mow around it — officially, this time. Thank you. Genuinely.

objectives.description: Hear Gerald out about the yard-related situation.
Ask Mrs. Pemberton what was here before the houses.
Get the flower an HOA variance — Form 44-Omega.
Bring Gerald the third variance in HOA history.

cleric.description: Dialogue-only, and the only W2 quest where the Cleric sees what others can't: 'This isn't magic. This is memory. The earth remembers the field. The flower is the memory expressing itself.'

unlocks_dialogue.note: Post-quest: Gerald's yard becomes a minor landmark — a single wildflower in a sea of regulation grass; neighbor NPCs occasionally stop to look at it.

cross_world_connection: W6: The Vertex's ground is uniform white except one small patch near the edge where a faint color persists — the exact color of Gerald's wildflower. With wildflower_variance in inventory, the Bard (Voice): 'Someone remembered the field all the way here.'
_wiring_notes: Step 2 emitter quest_world2_acceptable_variance_flower_examined: examine interact at the flower; any magic-capable party detects 'local persistence — established in a way normal flowers aren't. Older than the lawn. Possibly older than the neighborhood.' Mrs. Pemberton (new NPC, front porch next door, watching since before the HOA): 'That flower was there before the houses. This was all a field. Gerald's lawn is built on top of the field. The flower knows that. It keeps trying to remind the ground what the ground used to be.' Step 4 emitter quest_world2_acceptable_variance_variance_granted: Form 44-Omega bureaucracy at the community center (front-desk interact chain; granted-twice-in-history flavor in the clerk's reaction). DESIGN DOC ALTERNATES deliberately NOT modeled as quest paths in v1: 'remove the flower permanently' (high-tier spell) and 'do nothing' ('Alright. I'll just mow around it.') — the variance is the canonical quest resolution; the alternates can land later as decline-side dialogue outcomes without touching this file.
